title: The regime and its two questions
paras: Let's start with the regime itself, and its two questions. Advertisement control runs in parallel to mainstream planning, and it's built from pieces that are easily mistaken for one another. The Town and Country Planning Act 1990 supplies the skeleton. Sections 220 to 225 give the Secretary of State power to make regulations controlling advertisements "in the interests of amenity or public safety" — so the two grounds are written into the parent power itself. And section 222 deems planning permission granted for development involved in a display made in accordance with the Regulations, which is why sign and structure are dealt with under one regime. The flesh is the Control of Advertisements (England) Regulations 2007, in force from the 6th of April 2007, and England only — Wales runs on its own earlier set.

The statutory definition, in section 336, is deliberately enormous: any word, letter, model, sign, placard, board, notice, awning, blind, device or representation, illuminated or not, in the nature of and employed wholly or partly for the purposes of advertisement, announcement or direction. Now, "announcement or direction" means a sign that sells nothing is still caught — the solicitor's nameplate, the fingerpost to the village hall. And the definition includes any hoarding or similar structure used or adapted for the display of advertisements. So the empty frame, with no poster on it yet, is already an advertisement.

The edge of the definition is found in the words "in the nature of" — something has to be communicating a message to the public. The lighting cases illustrate the boundary from both sides. Illumination arranged to draw attention to premises has been treated as a sign, while ordinary or security floodlighting announces nothing — it's just light. And there's no statutory de minimis threshold. Relief for the trivial comes from the excepted and deemed classes, not from reading a "too small to count" exception into the definition.

The drafting is subtractive, which is why the Regulations read so oddly cold. Everything is defined in, and then whole categories are let out again through the Schedules. Schedule 1 lists the advertisements the main controls don't apply to; Schedule 3 lists the classes carrying deemed consent, with their conditions. Whatever is left over needs express consent. And whichever route brings a display to your desk, judgment is exercised on two grounds alone — amenity and public safety. They are fixed, exhaustive and statutory, which is why straying outside them isn't just poor practice — it's unlawful. National guidance now lives in the Planning Practice Guidance: Circular 03 of 2007 is withdrawn, though references to it survive in stale leaflets and old decisions.

title: Three doors, not a menu
paras: Three doors, then — not a menu. Every advertisement takes exactly one of three routes. It's excepted from control altogether, under Schedule 1. It carries deemed consent, granted in advance by the Regulations and subject to conditions. Or it requires express consent, by application. And they're a sequence, not a menu. Ask first whether the display is excepted — and if it is, stop. It's outside the controlled world, with no conditions to enforce and no application to demand. If not, ask whether it falls within a deemed class, and stays within that class's limits. Only a display that survives both questions needs express consent. Remember that exception and permission are different animals: the excepted sign has been left alone; the deemed sign has been let through on terms.

Three of the exceptions conceal traps. First, traffic signs. The exception frees signs prescribed or authorised under the road traffic legislation — it's a creature of authorisation, not of appearance. The brown-and-white "farm shop, next left" board that nobody authorised is not a traffic sign, however official it looks. It's an unlawful advertisement — and usually planted on highway land into the bargain. The genuine brown tourist sign is authorised against published criteria, and it confers no licence on the homemade copies pointing the last half-mile to the gate.

Second, enclosed land. Advertisements on enclosed land that aren't readily visible from outside it are excepted. But ownership isn't the test — visibility is — and "public place" reaches far beyond the carriageway: footpaths, bridleways, towpaths, parks, navigable water. So the sign inside the fenced compound that reads plainly from the footpath along the far boundary defeats the exception. And third, vehicles. An advertisement on a vehicle normally employed as a moving vehicle is outside control. The trailer towed into a lay-by and left as a billboard is not — it fixes the land beneath it as an advertisement site. And rotating identical trailers changes nothing, because what matters is the use of the site, not the identity of the vehicle.

Every exception is bounded, and crossing the limit doesn't shrink the exception — it extinguishes it. The display becomes an advertisement that was never excepted at all, and it must find deemed consent or apply. Location can also reshuffle the doors. In a conservation area, an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, a National Park or an Area of Special Control, deemed classes fall away or tighten — so a sign that's deemed on one high street needs express consent on another. The sign hasn't changed. Its setting has, and the setting has shut a door that would otherwise have been open.

title: Deemed consent and the everyday classes
paras: Now, to deemed consent, and the everyday classes. Deemed consent is the workhorse of the whole system. The Regulations grant consent in advance, in bulk, by class — and the great majority of lawful signage never saw an application. Two layers of condition sit on every class. The standard conditions apply across the scheme: clean, tidy and safe condition, careful removal when the time comes, and the site owner's permission before the display goes up. On the highway, that owner is the highway authority — a point on which many otherwise compliant displays fall down. Then each class carries its own ceilings, on size, height above ground, number, position and illumination. A display carries deemed consent only if it satisfies both layers.

The business premises class is the one officers are asked about most. A business may identify and advertise itself on its own premises, within limits built around the ground-floor fascia. Signs climbing above ground-floor level are treated far more restrictively — proliferating upper-storey advertising is exactly what coarsens a street — which is why the upper-floor occupier so often gets a disappointing answer. Illumination is treated more tightly than plain signage throughout, and the internally lit fascia that's unremarkable on a busy high street sits differently on a quiet parade. Then there are the functional classes — identification signs, direction signs about a site, forecourt and filling-station signage. Each grants a freedom that's generous for the purpose served, and lost the moment the sign stops identifying or directing and starts advertising to the passing road.

Estate agents' boards illustrate the whole pattern: one board per property, of limited size, on the property it relates to, removed within fourteen days after completion of the transaction. The lingering sold board is rarely an oversight — it's free advertising for the agent. And the board farm outside the student block, a dozen boards from several agents, has plainly outrun a class whose picture is one board for one property. Development signage is openly temporary: contractor boards for the construction period, marketing signs while there are genuinely unsold units — and neither survives the event that justified it. The "coming soon" board on a bare field, promoting a scheme that hasn't started, can't always shelter in classes built for works and sales actually happening.

Deemed consent ends in three ways, and they differ. A display that's enlarged, raised, multiplied or illuminated beyond its class steps outside the consent automatically — no bell rings, and the refit that crept a few inches taller has left the class whether or not anyone intended it. Time-limited classes expire with their event: the immaculate development hoarding on a sold-out scheme is out of consent not because it grew, but because its time is up. And the authority can deliberately remove deemed consent — a distinct, procedural power, which we'll come to alongside enforcement.

title: Express consent and the standard conditions
paras: So, to express consent and the standard conditions. A display arrives at express consent for one of two reasons. Either it never fell within any excepted or deemed class — the poster panel on the gable wall — or it had a class and left it: too big, too tall, too numerous, illuminated where the class doesn't allow, or standing where designation cut the class away. The machinery is familiar — form, fee, plans, decision, appeal — but the test is not. The application is determined on amenity and public safety alone, and a report that weighs need, content, competition, or the ordinary field of material considerations is building a decision that will not hold.

The application must describe a known thing: dimensions, position, height, materials — and above all, illumination. Not lit, externally lit, internally lit, static or changing. The two grounds turn entirely on the physical particulars, and an advertiser who's allowed to be vague about lighting may install something far brighter than anyone assessed. An applicant needn't own the land, and the consent confers no right over it. The site owner's permission is a separate matter — so a valid consent can still leave the advertiser with no right to erect the sign.

Every express consent carries the standard conditions automatically, whether they're mentioned or not. Bespoke conditions may be added only in service of the two grounds — limits on illumination, hours, luminance, colour or finish, or removal of an existing sign as the price of the new one. Conditions that reach for content — controlling what the advertisement says, or restricting it to particular messages — are exercised for a purpose the power does not extend to. For digital displays, the conditions control behaviour, not content: a minimum interval between image changes, no animation or transitions, a luminance cap that steps down after dark, and a default to blank or static on malfunction.

Consent runs for five years by default, with discretion for a shorter or longer period — and a shorter grant is sometimes the proportionate answer to a borderline display. At the end of the period there's no cliff edge. The display may ordinarily continue on the same terms without a fresh application, unless and until the authority acts through the proper machinery. Changing the sign — enlarging, relighting, repositioning — is a new display, and it needs a new application. And the authority may decline to determine a bare repeat: a similar application refused in the recent past, with no significant change in the relevant considerations since. Declining isn't refusing. It's a decision not to engage, and it carries no fresh right of appeal.

title: Assessment: what works and what never does
paras: Assessment, then — what works, and what never does. Amenity is not a property of the sign. It's a relationship between the sign and its setting, and the same advertisement can be acceptable on one frontage and harmful on another with nothing about the sign changed. The assessment reads the setting honestly — the busy commercial street, the coherent historic parade, the open country — then weighs size, proportion to the building, position on the elevation, height, materials and illumination, all together, against it. Harm is expressed in terms of fit and relationship, not taste. "Out of proportion to the modest frontage it dominates" is an amenity judgment; "ugly" is not. And illumination demands the question be asked twice, because a sign that's modest by day can dominate by night.

Public safety is narrower, and more evidence-hungry. It's overwhelmingly road safety, and it requires a located hazard — what is it about this sign, in this position, on this stretch of road, that creates danger? The junction, the bend, the crossing it draws the eye from, the signal it competes with. Conspicuity is not danger — every advertisement is meant to be noticed. The highway authority's view is central in both directions: a safety case it supports is strong; one it declines to support is in serious difficulty. Distraction arguments have content where a demanding location meets a sign that actively works to hold attention — dense, puzzling, or changing.

Clutter is the harm done by accumulation — no single sign refusable, but the sum a degraded scene — and it's a legitimate amenity ground, judged as the present effect of this sign added to what's already there. The trap is refusing on proliferation alone. "If we allow this, we must allow others" is a speculative precedent argument, and it fails, because each future application will be judged on its own merits when it comes. "This sign, in this already busy frontage, worsens a cumulative harm that is real now" — that succeeds. The excluded arguments all fail for one reason. Need — which cuts both ways, because no business case can condemn a harmless sign either — competition, content and taste are simply not among the two grounds, however sincerely they're pressed.

Sensitive settings change the threshold, not the test. In conservation areas, Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty and National Parks, the decision is still amenity and safety — but judged against a baseline the system has identified as specially valuable, and the deemed classes are cut back, so more needs express consent. The Area of Special Control goes further. It's a designation made by the advertisement regime itself — characteristically rural, often decades old and half-forgotten — within which some categories, the hoarding, the large freestanding panel, are prohibited outright, so that no application could succeed. And the listed building runs a parallel consent. Advertisement consent, deemed or express, does not dispense with listed building consent for fixing a sign to a listed building, and satisfying one regime does nothing to answer the other.

title: Enforcement: the offence, discontinuance and fly-posting
paras: Finally, enforcement — the offence, discontinuance, and fly-posting. Displaying an advertisement without the consent it requires is a criminal offence, and a continuing one: a fine on conviction, with a further daily sum while the display remains. Liability is drawn wide. It reaches those whose goods, trade or business are advertised, so the authority can pursue the party with the real interest, rather than the untraceable person who wielded the paste — subject to defences for those who can show the display was without their knowledge, or that they took reasonable steps. Prosecutions are nonetheless rare — slow, formal, uncertain, and competing for scarce enforcement capacity against breaches that feel more urgent — and most practical enforcement is achieved by the letter that carries, unspoken, the credible possibility of one.

The discontinuance notice reaches where the offence cannot: it brings a lawful deemed-consent display to an end. The threshold is deliberately heightened — discontinuance must be necessary to remedy a substantial injury to the amenity of the locality, or a danger to members of the public — because the power extinguishes an existing lawful right. And it works in two stages. Served through the proper procedure, with a period before effect and a right of appeal, it removes the lawfulness rather than the sign. A display continued after the notice bites is then an ordinary unlawful display, and the offence is engaged.

Withdrawal of deemed consent works at the level of the rule, not the display. It removes a class of deemed consent, for a particular case or across a defined area, so that displays of that kind need express consent in future. It isn't prohibition — the application can still be made, and granted on the merits — and the estate agents' board in the blighted terrace is the textbook use. Fly-posting and highway signs are often better handled outside the advertisement regime altogether. The defacement powers secure removal or obliteration of the material without first winning a contest about identity. And a sign on the verge, the footway or a lamp column sits on the highway authority's own land, where its powers over unauthorised structures and obstructions are frequently the fastest route to removal.

The digital, L-E-D and changing-image display is the sharpest current test, and the regime copes because it keys off effect rather than technology. The screen is judged on amenity and safety exactly as a painted board is — but it engages both grounds more intensely: one installation carrying a dozen advertisers, brightness that transforms the night scene, and a changing image that draws the eye back repeatedly — which, beside a fast road, is the located-hazard analysis at its most serious. The control is in the conditioning: dwell time, no animation, luminance capped and stepped down after dark, failure defaulting to blank. Behaviour — never content.
